summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Runny nose and congestion, with associated ear pulling and irritability.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports onset of runny nose and congestion approximately 24 hours ago. He also reports ear pulling, which he states has been present since the onset of nasal symptoms. He denies fever, but reports feeling irritable and difficulty sleeping. He has been drinking approximately 7 ounces of formula this morning and only 3 ounces of formula this morning. He has been using saline mist, baby Vick's on his feet, and a humidifier. His older sister has been sick. He has been using a cradle cap brush with aveno shampoo. He reports that his appetite has been poor, and he has been fighting to go to sleep. He has been using zarbee's for the stuffy nose.

Findings
The patient's temperature was 100.3°F rectally. Examination reveals clear nasal mucus. Examination of the ears reveals no acute findings. Vitals are within normal limits. No physical exam findings beyond those described in the history.

Assessment
Viral upper respiratory infection is the most likely diagnosis. The patient's symptoms are consistent with a common cold.

Plan
Continue saline mist and humidifier. Administer half a teaspoon of the patient's sister's medication if a barky cough develops. Follow up in one week if symptoms do not improve or worsen. Order a follow-up appointment in one week. Provide instructions to the parents regarding the use of zarbee's and the importance of avoiding honey until one year of age. Provide instructions regarding the use of the cradle cap brush and aveno shampoo. Provide instructions regarding the use of head & shoulder's, selsun blue, or nizoral ad for the cradle cap.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

section_miscategorizations.text: No physical exam findings beyond those described in the history.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence references 'those described in the history,' which blurs the boundary between subjective history and objective exam. While it is summarizing the absence of additional exam findings (thus acceptable in Findings), the wording could mislead a reader about where certain details were obtained. This is an organizational issue rather than a true section error.
